nativeRange.habitat: Marshes, swampy lowlands, ditches, and the muddy margins of slow-moving streams across tropical East and Southeast Asia and northern Australia. The species grows both emersed (terrestrial/marginal swamp form) and submerged.

decorPreferences: high-PAR full-spectrum lighting for red-purple coloration
iron and micronutrient dosing for vibrant color
ample vertical space (40-60 cm) for the bushy mature form

summary: A demanding red-stem background plant from Asia and northern Australia, prized for purple-to-orange star-whorl foliage when given high light, CO2, and steady iron dosing.
careNotes:
Pogostemon stellatus is a demanding red stem plant from the swamps and slow-moving waters of East and Southeast Asia and northern Australia, belonging to the mint family (Lamiaceae). It is sold under several names: the older genus Eustralis stellata (also spelled Eusteralis stellata) remains in trade circulation, and Star Stellatus is a common retail label. Two main forms are widely available: the 'Broad Leaf' type, with wider leaves arranged in classic star-shaped whorls, and 'Octopus', a narrow-whorl variant with thinner, more sprawling foliage that is generally regarded as somewhat less demanding and more reliably green to light-pink than the type form.

Light and CO2 are the primary levers for coloration. Under low light without supplemental CO2, the plant tends toward pale green and may shed lower leaves over time. Under high light with pressurized CO2 injection it develops the vivid purple-to-orange star whorls that make it a centerpiece background plant in high-tech planted tanks. Achieving and holding that coloration is why this species is considered advanced — survival is achievable in moderate conditions, but the coloration most buyers are after requires consistent high-light, CO2, and careful fertilization.

Pogostemon stellatus is regarded by experienced planted-tank keepers as a reliable indicator of iron and micronutrient levels: pale or yellowing leaf tips more often point to iron or trace-element deficiency than to a shortage of the major nutrients nitrogen or phosphorus. A commonly used approach for the deepest red-purple expression is to keep nitrate low (around 5-10 ppm) while dosing iron and full micronutrients generously. The plant is also known for occasionally stalling growth even under seemingly good conditions; trimming back to fresh tops and replanting the cuttings typically restarts vigorous growth. Best placed as a background hedge or rear-midground bush in aquariums at least 60 cm long with ample vertical space for the bushy mature form.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings. Cut the apical 10-15 cm from a mature stem and replant the cutting directly into nutrient-rich substrate, where it will root quickly. The mother stem responds to topping by producing lateral side shoots, which can themselves be trimmed and replanted for rapid mass propagation. Floating cuttings will root in the water column before being planted.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Used for species identity, native range (Asia), height 15-30+ cm, growth rate (medium), light demand (high), CO2 demand (high/required), care difficulty (advanced/difficult), placement (mid-to-background bush), color behavior (low NO3/PO4 yields purple-orange, high yields green), iron/micronutrient indicator role, and propagation by lateral-shoot cuttings. The supplied search URL (tropica.com/en/search?q=Pogostemon+stellatus) returned no per-species content; resolved to the species' detail page (item 053G).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked for the 'Octopus' trade variant (narrow-whorl form). Confirms background placement, fast growth in good conditions, stem-cutting propagation, low-to-medium light tolerance for the 'Octopus' form (notably less demanding than the type 'Broad Leaf' form), and use as cover for fish/shrimp.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Confirms family Lamiaceae, Asian origin, height 10-20" (~25-50 cm), pH 6.5-7.5, care 'easy' (for survival), moderate-to-high light for red coloration, CO2 helpful but not strictly required, medium growth, mid/background placement, color range green-pink-red driven by iron. Also confirms the synonym Eusteralis stellata is still in commercial use.

additional.name: Aquasabi
additional.notes: Confirms family Lamiaceae, native range (East/Southeast Asia and northern Australia swamps), height 15-60 cm, temperature 15-32 °C (prefers 24-28), pH 5-7, gH 0-30, kH 1-18, fast growth, high light essential, CO2 essential, care difficulty 'difficult', background/midground placement, fertilization targets (NO3 5-20 mg/l, PO4 0.2-2 mg/l), and the species' role as an iron/micronutrient deficiency indicator. Used as the primary parameter source for averaging.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Confirms temperature 22-28 °C (72-82 °F), pH 6.0-7.5, gH 2-12, kH 1-6, moderate care difficulty (5/10), medium-to-fast growth, midground-to-background placement, nutrient-rich substrate, and the existence of the 'Octopus' variant (described as less demanding and more sprawling than the type form). Cross-source disagreement on max height (cites up to 90 cm) — averaged out in the height field.
